data.title: Blackbaud: Growth Rates Deteriorate Further In Q4, Invest Elsewhere
data.description:
In early 2025, news headlines have been swamped with President Trump's raft of executive orders. The changes brought by the new administration have been sweeping and broad, but beyond the remaking of the executive branch, Trump has also succeeded at enacting cultural changes across corporate boardrooms, particularly by pushing companies down DEI and ESG initiatives that sprouted during the pandemic period.

Amid this backdrop, Blackbaud (NASDAQ:BLKB) has suffered. The company has a broad suite of tools for its education, not-for-profit, and public sector clientele, but a major area for growth had been providing software for ESG-style initiatives in corporations. Stacked on top of a tough macro environment, the company's growth has slid to near-zero, as evidenced by its recent Q4 earnings release. Shares of Blackbaud dropped ~10% after the print, as investors lost patience for this fading story.

Major risks cloud this company as growth slows to a halt

I last wrote a bearish note on Blackbaud in November, when the stock was trading closer to $85 per share. Since then, Blackbaud has eroded a further ~20% (we note that the company's decision to not take an $80 take-private offer now appears unwise), while growth rates have soured further. With this in mind, I'm reiterating my sell rating on Blackbaud.

There are a number of risk factors that investors need to keep in mind here, beyond just the unfavorable social and political climate for its ESG growth initiatives. To me, Blackbaud's growth strategy is flawed. Historically, the company has relied heavily on M&A for its growth. The legacy of this strategy is a very constrained balance sheet: the company has just $67.6 million of cash left on its balance sheet, counterbalanced by $1.08 billion of debt. This ~$1 billion of net debt, meanwhile, stands at a ~10x leverage ratio against its TTM adjusted EBITDA of just $102 million (note that commercial banks typically consider a ~3x leverage ratio to be a highly leveraged, risky company).

Meanwhile, its acquisitions also don't have a great track record of panning out: it just sold one of its education subsidiaries, EVERFI, and took a major ~$0.4 billion impairment charge as a result.

We also note that Blackbaud's revenue growth relies on an updated business model and pricing strategy that effectively amounts to a pricing increase. The company is now renewing its customers' contracts to three-year terms (versus a mix of term lengths previously), implementing a "mid to high teens" rate increase at renewal with a "mid to high single digit" increase in both years 2 and 3.

Needless to say, these are onerous terms. Organizations are struggling to keep budgets slim in today's macro environment. If we take the example of a 15%, 7%, and 7% price increase over a three-year timeframe, a customer would be paying ~30% more for Blackbaud by the third year: to me, this may be a driver that causes more customer churn. Blackbaud's failure to grow beyond the low single digits suggests that churn may be outweighing the benefits of its price increases.

All in all, this is a company on very shaky footing - and in my view, the post-earnings crash has quite a ways to go still.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Blackbaud's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Blackbaud's revenue grew at just 2.4% y/y to $302.2 million, missing Wall Street's expectations of $303.4 million (+2.8% y/y) while also decelerating a point from Q3's 3.3% growth rate. We note that organic growth was stronger when excluding the impact of the sale of EVERFI, standing at 3.2% y/y - still, this is not exactly the kind of growth we'd expect or hope for, especially from a company that is implementing rather extreme price increases.

The bridge below helps to explain the drivers behind Blackbaud's poor growth:

The company's social sector drove growth, both through the company's implementation of price increases (reflected in contractual recurring revenue) as well as higher take rates on transactional recurring revenue. Take rates represent the percentage that Blackbaud is earning from helping its clients process gifts and other transactions, and higher take rates is yet another element of a price increase - in other words, this is not a growth lever that can be pulled in perpetuity. Meanwhile, we note that the company's corporate sector revenue declined -14% y/y, which is fitting in an era when many companies are slashing ESG initiatives.

Meanwhile, on the profitability front, the company has been able to barely hang on its current margin profile. Adjusted EBITDA in Q4 rose 3.1% y/y to $102.2 million, with margins of 33.8% improving just 20bps y/y versus 33.6% in the year-ago Q4.

The company is expecting profitability to improve by 170bps y/y in FY25, which I believe to be optimistic. Its current pace of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion already factors in the macroeconomic headwinds as well as benefits from recent price increases. Yes, the company will save opex from divesting the underperforming EVERFI segment, but this may not be enough to offset inflation pressures and the possibility of added churn. I'd prefer to see additional cost-saving measures on Blackbaud's part to become more comfortable with this name.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $72, Blackbaud trades at a market cap of $3.66 billion. After we net off the $67.6 million of cash and $1.08 billion of debt on the company's balance sheet, Blackbaud's resulting enterprise value is $4.67 billion.

Meanwhile, for the current fiscal year FY25, Blackbaud has guided to $1.115-$1.125 billion in revenue (~5% y/y growth), $190 million in midpoint FCF (a 17% FCF margin), and $4.16-$4.35 in pro forma EPS (2-7% EPS growth, which also is supported by the company's buybacks, with ~$0.6 billion remaining on its current authorization).

I'm skeptical that the company can achieve ~5% organic revenue growth when it exited Q4 at a growth rate that is two points lower. But if we take the company's outlook at face value, Blackbaud trades at multiples of:

  • 4.2x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 25x EV/FY25 FCF
  • 17x P/E

To me, despite a mountain of operational and fundamental risks that Blackbaud faces, the stock isn't even quite a value stock, with its P/E multiple just barely below the broader S&P 500 (and we note that the company's earnings multiple would be bloated higher if we account for its debt, which is why its ~25x FCF multiple is a more accurate representation).

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: January's $10 Billion M&A Deals And Bids Span Sectors
data.description:
A US power plant operator and a pharmaceutical company were among the M&A targets in transactions valued at more than $10 billion that were announced in January.

There were five potential $10 billion-plus transactions initiated worldwide in January, excluding terminated bids, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data. That matched the three-month totals recorded in the second and third quarters of 2024. The fourth quarter of 2024 saw twice as many $10 billion-plus deal announcements, with 10.

The January megadeals included transactions in the energy, financials, pharmaceuticals and materials sectors, signaling a broad-based large-cap M&A market appetite.

Announced deals

On Jan. 10, renewable electricity company Constellation Energy Generation LLC (CEG) agreed to acquire power plant operator Calpine Corp. in a transaction valued at $29.39 billion at the time of the announcement. A few days later, pharmaceutical giant Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and biopharmaceutical company Intra-Cellular Therapies Inc. (ITCI) signed a definitive agreement confirming Johnson & Johnson's proposed $13.65 billion purchase of Intra-Cellular Therapies on Jan. 13.

The combination of Constellation Energy Generation and Calpine Corp. would create the US's largest clean energy provider, according to the merger announcement. The deal adds geographic diversity to Maryland-based Constellation Energy Generation's coverage by bringing Texas-based Calpine Corp.'s customers under its umbrella.

Johnson & Johnson's acquisition of Intra-Cellular Therapies, which focuses on the development and commercialization of therapeutics for central nervous system disorders, demonstrates the company's commitment to research on neuropsychiatric and neurodegenerative disorders, Chairman and CEO Joaquin Duato said in Johnson & Johnson's press release announcing the deal.

Australian materials companies Aston Minerals Ltd. (OTCPK:WMNNF) and Torque Metals Ltd. agreed to a merger of equals Jan. 28 valued at $13.09 billion at the announcement, including the acquisition of outstanding options, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data. According to the press release announcing the deal, Torque Metals will acquire 100% of the fully paid ordinary shares in Aston Minerals and 100% of the unlisted Aston Minerals options. Excluding the options transaction, the deal's total consideration to shareholders at the announcement was AU$14.4 million.

Placed bids

Multiple potential acquirers placed large bids during the month of January. Software company QXO Inc. (QXO) offered to acquire materials distribution company Beacon Roofing Supply Inc. (BECN) in a tender offer with an implied transaction value of $11.55 billion at the time of announcement on Jan. 15. Italian bank Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena SpA (OTCPK:BMDPF) submitted a $13.73 all-stock takeover offer to its peer Mediobanca Banca di Credito Finanziario SpA (OTCPK:MDIBF) on Jan. 24. Both bids were rejected by the targets' boards.

QXO commenced a tender offer to purchase all outstanding Beacon Roofing Supply shares for $124.25 per share in cash. Beacon Roofing Supply confirmed the receipt of the offer Nov. 11, 2024, and rejected it. The board stated in a press release that QXO's bid severely undervalued Beacon Roofing Supply for growth and future value creation.

QXO responded with a letter to Beacon Roofing Supply on Feb. 10, stating the bid represents a 37% premium to Beacon Roofing Supply's 90-day unaffected share price of $91.02 per share as of Nov. 15, 2024.

Monte dei Paschi's bid to purchase Mediobanca comes amid a pickup in bank M&A in Italy. The Italian government backed the bid, with both the country's Prime Minister, Giorgia Meloni, and Economy Minister Giancarlo Giorgetti saying it would strengthen Italy's economy and banking system. Mediobanca's board rejected Monte dei Paschi's bid on Jan. 28, stating in a press release that the board found the offer "devoid of industrial and financial rationale and... therefore destructive."

If the proposed purchase was successful, it would be the largest Italian bank deal of the decade, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NewLake Capital Partners: 11% Yield, Discount To Book Value Make Up For Risks
data.description:
NewLake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP), a cannabis REIT, has been performing poorly since my last Buy rating on the stock on October 25, 2024, with a total return of -18.4%. However, the business was still doing just fine as of Q3, which is its most recently reported earnings period. Now, the stock is trading under its tangible book value, and it yields nearly 11%, presenting an attractive opportunity for dividend investors.

At the same time, I believe tenant risk is moderate, as the top two tenants make up a large portion of revenues, and several of NLCP's tenants don't have ideal financials. Case in point, NLCP has had problems with two of its tenants, as they struggled to make rent payments in recent months.

But then I also consider that some of this downside potential is likely baked into the valuation and that the company has a high-quality business model, operating in a growing industry. You can see more of my thoughts on its business (as well as thoughts on the SAFER Banking Act) in my previous article. There's also the fact that its high-yielding dividend is covered by AFFO, at least for now.

After balancing these pros and cons, I'm still leaning bullish on NLCP stock, giving it a Buy rating.

Two Of NLCP's Tenants Are In Trouble

Two of NLCP's tenants have been struggling to pay their leases recently. Revolutionary Clinics is one of these tenants, making up 5.7% of the NewLake Capital Partner's annualized base rent (ABR) as of the third quarter.

The company paid only 50% of its contractual rent in Q3 and continued paying the reduced amount into October as well. We'll get more updates on the status of the company after NLCP reports earnings on March 6. But for now, things aren't looking great because Revolutionary's main lender "asked a judge to place the company in receivership," per greenmarketreport.com.

Next, there's Calypso, which made up 6.5% of NLCP's annualized base rent as of Q3. Calypso didn't pay rent for both September and October 2024. To try to improve the situation, NLCP used about $299,000 from Calypso's escrow deposit to cover the missed rent from September. It also suspended its obligation to "fund the remaining improvement allowance of approximately $1.0 million until all outstanding rent is paid, and the escrow deposit is replenished."

On the bright side, in the Q3 earnings call, NewLake's management said the company is confident in the demand for Calypso's facility in Pennsylvania, which indicates to me that they may not struggle too much to find another tenant. Here's the quote: "Calypso is evaluating all options for the business, and we are confident there is demand for the high-quality facility in a state with positive tailwinds."

Considering that Pennsylvania was the 6th-biggest cannabis retail market in the US (according to this article from September) despite only having medical-related sales, perhaps it's true that there is demand for the facility.

Taking A Look At The Rest Of NLCP's Tenants

I believe NLCP's tenant profile presents above-average risk, but this should come to no surprise since it's a cannabis REIT. Let's analyze each tenant briefly.

The biggest tenant presents a mixed picture: Its biggest tenant is Curaleaf (OTCPK:CURLF), which represented 22.9% of annualized base rent. That much rent coming from one company is already a risk, but let's take a look into Curaleaf's financials.

Curaleaf's interest coverage ratio is 0.4x, meaning that its EBIT doesn't cover interest payments. That's a red flag, but there's a silver lining.

While its interest coverage ratio is 0.4x, its free cash flow is positive and expected to improve in 2025. Curaleaf's TTM free cash flow comes in at around $47.6 million, and this figure is expected to reach $67 million for FY2024 and $114.5 million for 2025, per the consensus of two analysts on tikr.com.

Thus, while I'd like to see the interest coverage ratio improve, Curaleaf should be able to pay its rent if its free cash flow stays positive. Further, with its market cap being rather large for a cannabis stock ($1.1 billion), the firm is likely big enough to be able to raise a notable amount of money via an equity raise if it has to. This can help with rent/debt payments.

The 2nd-biggest tenant is not so bad: Cresco Labs (OTCQX:CRLBF) represents 13% of ABR, so it's worth paying attention to. The good news is that this company doesn't have a problem with its interest coverage ratio. Cresco Labs' TTM interest coverage ratio is 2.0x, and it has been around that level for a few quarters now. Its EBIT is expected to be $114 million for 2024 and $113.65 million for 2025. That's a good buffer when you consider that Cresco Labs' TTM interest expense is $60.162 million.

This company is FCF positive as well. Its TTM free cash flow is $77.6 million, and based on the estimates of five analysts, FCF of $96.7 million is expected for FY2024 and $43 million for 2025. Assuming these estimates are at least somewhat accurate, then I'm not too worried about Cresco Labs.

Trulieve Cannabis (OTCQX:TCNNF), the third-largest tenant: Trulieve represents 10.8% of ABR. My thoughts on Trulieve are similar to those of Cresco Labs. It has positive FCF ($283.1 million in the past 12 months), and its interest coverage ratio of 1.8x is not horrible. Its free cash flow is expected to dip to $129.3 million for FY2024 and then rise to $156.6 million by the end of this year. This means the company should be able to pay rent.

The fourth-largest publicly traded tenant doesn't look healthy, but there's a chance: The next tenant is The Cannabist Company (OTCQX:CBSTF) (CBST:CA), representing 8.2% of ABR. This is probably the worst one so far, as its TTM EBIT is ~$300 thousand, but its interest expense was $44.9 million. Further, its free cash flow is negative, at -$24.147 million for the past 12 months. Adding to this shaky financial position, its cash and short-term investments are only $32.68 million, while the total debt comes in at $478.2 million when counting capital leases.

The silver lining is that 2025 free cash flow for the company is forecast at $16.2 million. However, that's just according to one analyst, so it's a big "we'll see."

The remaining tenants: Now that I've some of the bigger tenants out of the way, let's briefly talk about the smaller ones. I will ignore the private companies, as there's not enough information on them.

Acreage Holdings (OTCQX:ACRDF), the fifth-largest publicly traded tenant, doesn't look great. It makes up 6.4% of ABR, has negative free cash flow, a large debt load, and not much cash. However, it was acquired by Canopy USA, so it can potentially be stronger as a combined company.

The last one I'll talk about is Ayr Wellness (OTCQX:AYRWF), representing 5.8% of ABR as of Q3. This company had -$9.82 million in FCF for the past 12 months, with an interest coverage ratio of -0.3x. Again, though, this is another company where analysts expect improvements. Three analysts estimate its 2025 free cash flow to reach $9.6 million. It's a mixed picture.

As you can see, NLCP doesn't have the best tenants, but my analysis leads me to believe that the top three tenants, which make up 46.7% of ABR, won't have trouble making their rent payments anytime soon, unless analysts' estimates are way off. If you're comfortable with concentration risk and tenant risk, then buying NLCP stock at the current valuation can potentially be rewarding.

NLCP Stock Yields Nearly 11%, Trades Under Tangible Book Value

Now, let's talk about the dividend and the valuation. At current levels, it yields about 11%, with an annualized dividend of $1.72/share. Additionally, the dividend is covered by AFFO, as the AFFO payout ratio was 84% in Q3. This gives the company a decent amount of room to maneuver any drop in earnings without having to cut the dividend.

If the company can maintain this dividend, which is possible since the top tenants aren't bad and the 84% AFFO payout ratio gives some wiggle room for the bad ones, then 11% returns are pretty decent. And if the company grows over time as the cannabis market grows, then the dividend can potentially slowly increase over the years. After all, if we use this conservative estimate from Statista, the US cannabis market is expected to grow at a 2.24% CAGR from 2025-29. That's not much, but it's still growth, and it can help sustain companies like NLCP over the long run.

Moving on to its tangible book value. Per FinBox, NLCP's tangible book value per share is $18.59. That means that the stock trades at 0.84x tangible book value if using its last share price of $15.69. And if you use NLCP's book value per share (not tangible) of $19.48, then the stock trades at 0.81x this level. Similar to the AFFO payout ratio at 84%, this valuation gives you a margin of safety, allowing you to buy the stock under its tangible net worth.

The Takeaway

While NewLake Capital Partners isn't the "safest" investment, it's a high-yield way to get exposure to the growing cannabis market. Not all of NLCP's tenants are financially sound, and some are in trouble. However, the top three tenants, which make up almost half of annualized rent, are not bad. With the stock yielding 11% (covered by AFFO) and now trading at 0.84x tangible book value and 0.81x "regular" book value, I think that a good chunk of the downside risk is priced in.

I don't recommend going big on this stock yet (I'd prefer an even bigger margin of safety), but it's good enough to warrant a Buy rating for those who want a double-digit yield and are comfortable with the risks I've presented.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Korn Ferry: More Resilient Yet Cheaper Than Peers
data.description:
Korn Ferry (NYSE:KFY) is in a very different environment from when we last covered them, at the tail end of the hiring frenzy going on in the corporate world during COVID-19. The situation is that they themselves are doing quite a lot of layoffs for the benefit of their bottom line, struggling as the dynamics in hiring and firing pressure their businesses on the demand side. We believe the current situation, particularly as it concerns the US market, is going to be rather durable. While not great, the US situation will be durably better than elsewhere. KFY is more indexed to the US than some peers (a good thing), and we believe they are the marquee pick in the comp spread as the player with the largest sales and a significant and monetizable digital platform and repository. We don't see why they should trade at a discount to peers operating in more beleaguered markets. Therefore, there is a relative value case, but the end market situation has us staying away.

Latest Earnings

As a note, technically the company recognizes both fee and non-fee revenue (license and subscription revenue technically on their IP). However, almost all revenue is just fee revenue, and this fact is useful to keep in mind when reading their disclosure because it can be puzzling initially when they make quite irrelevant reference to growth in non-fee revenue.

The situation in KFY is pretty straightforward and acts as a barometer for the overall dynamics going on in the labor market. There are slight revenue declines YoY of around 4%. They are making headway into Europe, including through acquisitions, and European executive search was one of the few areas, in addition to APAC executive search, where they achieved any YoY revenue growth. This will likely not last into the coming quarters as European peers guide lower, and it's already been an idiosyncratic and share-gaining performance by KFY to have achieved revenue growth in EMEA at all.

Less resilient was professional search as opposed to executive search. While professional search for permanent employees was only down around 6% YoY, just slightly below the group average, the component of professional search associated to interim (contract and temporary) professionals was down significantly more at -12%, better than last quarter but still bad.

Another way they cut up the business is into new business components in the segments, where it is measured how revenue growth has been going at newly won customers. Unsurprisingly, new business typically means KFY has not fully exploited the customer wallet yet, and YoY figures are more or less flat as opposed to decline on a consolidated basis, where an above-group-average performance is to be expected.

Consulting and interim professional search work is an attempt to get into a larger addressable market. General organizational consulting starts getting you into the territory of management consulting, and general interim staffing services are a different and also large market given the extent of contract work in the corporate world. Moreover, secularly, it is also reasonable to expect growth in contract work merely as companies find ways to make less committal relationships with the labor force, and the labor force looks to be more mercenary and have more freedom in how they sell their skills. In other words, a surge and swing workforce to more easily supplement the permanent base. However, the general economic climate is not lending itself in particular to the latter at this moment, even if there might be a secular case.

As seen in some examples from our coverage universe, dynamics are of slow hiring but also slow firing of permanent employees, where in attempt to avoid severance costs and other frictions, companies are looking to use return to office mandates and other methodologies to get unwanted employees to head to the exits themselves or tolerate keeping their current stable. Therefore, much of the cost-cutting work being done to counteract the inflation we've been dealing with for the past three years is focused on temporary work. These dynamics are evident in the US, but a weaker and more political fraught European economy means that reluctance to turn over jobs is even more of an issue there, and has been taking a turn for the worse even now with recent profit warnings in for more Europe-indexed peers.

In fact, the need to cut costs concerns KFY itself, which aimed to cut the workforce by almost 10%, and has managed to raise EBITDA margins significantly, by 340 bps, in the process. That was enough to lead to growth in adjusted EBITDA overall by 19%. Productivity of employees should be improving, and are certainly being used more efficiently based on the EBITDA improvements with less real estate, non-comp expenses and back office needed to house the existing workforce.

Bottom Line

While we think that the factors such as inflation remain an enduring issue in the US, and therefore are inclined to believe that an improvement in labour market dynamics is not imminent, we do think that there is quite a strong relative case for KFY since its peers are in more trouble than KFY are. We aren't terribly interested in the direction of the KFY end markets, but we note that on an EV/EBITDA and, more importantly, a PE basis, KFY trades at a significant discount to peers both with the same geographic indexation (around 50:50) between the US and the rest of the world and those with less exposure to a relatively more resilient US hiring market. Average EV/EBITDA on a forward basis is around 8.2x, with KFY at 6.5x, and PE at around 27x against KFY at 14x. While there is slow hiring and firing in the US, employment has been much harder to find in Northern and Eastern Europe, which includes the UK and the bulk of the Eurozone countries. European peers have been issuing profit warnings, and significant further declines, as much as 30% in EBITDA, are fully expected into the coming year. Some of this is due to economy, but also political upheaval as politics in Europe moves to the right and concerns around government coffers and workforces. People are reluctant to turn over their jobs, and that's quite understandable. Economic data in the EU is also more fragile, though an end to the war in Ukraine, with the US pursuing a deal with Russia, might improve things.

While KFY may take around a $40 million hit due to how the holidays fell this year and also the innate fact that end of year holidays mean time off from work (seasonal effect), there aren't any exceptionally concerning dynamics in the US market, at least incrementally. EMEA exposure at KFY is rather low, at a little over 20% of the executive search revenues and less than 10% of overall revenues. KFY is not terribly exposed, as indicated by the profit warnings coming out of some of KFY's UK-headquartered peers, like Hays plc (OTCPK:HAYPF). With the guide pointing to straight sailing, at least on a sequential basis, into the Q3 adjusting for seasonal effects, KFY is looking like the relatively resilient pick in the spread.

data.title: Welltower Stock: Long-Term Potential Vs. Near-Term Risks
data.description:
Welltower (NYSE:WELL) stock has been on an excellent run over the past year, surging close to 61%, while leaving its competition in the dust. Though some of that enthusiasm stems from the broader market tailwinds, the bulk of it is driven by Welltower's outstanding operational performance. Its recent quarterlies have shown impressive performances in its senior housing segment, driven by healthy occupancy gains and margin expansion. Nonetheless, its current valuation, at over 100 times forward earnings, suggests that a lot of that upside is already priced in. Also, my dividend valuation model (DVM) supports that notion, with a fair value that is significantly lower than its current price. Hence, it's best to wait for a better entry point before loading up more on the stock.

Breaking Down the Business: Key Segments & Strategy

Welltower is a large-cap real estate investment trust (REIT) specializing in senior housing and medical office buildings in the U.S. and internationally. It owns more than 2,100 properties with close to 200,000 units, focusing on a market that benefits from scale and geographic density.

It makes its money by owning and leasing healthcare properties by essentially acting as a landlord collecting rent from operators running these facilities. Additionally, it also takes a cut of the profits from residents' monthly payments.

The chart above offers a breakdown of Welltower's key operating segments based on revenues. Seniors Housing Operating or SHOP is the largest contributor to company sales at roughly 72%. This is also where Welltower is witnessing the strongest growth, with the segment posting a 24% same‐store net operating income (SSNOI) growth, which was the ninth consecutive quarter of 20%+ growth. As we look ahead, the segment is poised to remain a massive long-term growth driver, backed by favorable demographics and ongoing operational improvements.

Secondly, we have Triple-Net Leases contributing 16% to the overall pie, which saw NOI growth of around 5% in Q4, which is now comfortably ahead of pre-pandemic levels. Third in line is Outpatient Medical, which accounts for roughly 11% of operating sales. While it's the more "boring" side of the business, occupancy remains in the mid-90% range, with Q4 same-store NOI growth at 2% to 3%.

As we look ahead, Welltower remains in an enviable position to capture the cyclical and secular growth momentum in an aging population that continues driving demand for senior and healthcare services. Consider the stats, b y 2030, 1 in 5 Americans will be of retirement age (65+). That demographic could potentially expand to 95 million by 2060, which reflects a much larger group that needs assisted living. Moreover, according to Market Research Future, the assisted living facility market could grow from $4.1 billion in 2024 to $7.25 billion by 2032, representing a 7.40% CAGR. Hence, the company is in a position for long-term robust growth, ensuring ongoing momentum in its expansion.

Breaking Down the Numbers: Welltower's Financials in Focus

Welltower has been a standout performer, as shown by its impressive AFFO metrics. Over the past three years, we've seen its AFFO advance by 9%, more than double the broader sector's growth. What's even more impressive is its forward AFFO growth at over 15%, comfortably ahead of the sector median at just under 2%. A few other metrics that underscore its growth story include the YOY expansion in OCF, which has surged by more than 40%, ahead of the sector's 4% median increase. Additionally, forward OCF growth remains solid at 26%, head-and-shoulders above the sector median. Also, Welltower's EPS diluted growth has soared almost 140% YOY, beating the sector's 1.15%, highlighting the management's success in driving bottom-line expansion.

Over the past 11 consecutive quarters, Welltower has bested analyst expectations on the bottom line while falling short on sales estimates three times. Its normalized FFO per share of $1.13 surpassed estimates by a cent and marked a solid improvement from the $0.96 recorded a year ago. Total sales jumped to $2.25 billion, beating the $2.19 billion consensus, and comparing favorably to the $1.75 billion posted in Q4 last year.

The key driver for the company was the 12.8% YOY increase in the total portfolio (SSNOI), spearheaded by the 24% SSNOI bump in its SHOP segment, which was the ninth quarter of 20%+ growth. Sequential occupancy gains came in at a heartening 120 basis points, despite the seasonal slowdowns. Compensation per occupied room rose just 1.2% YOY, leading to an operating margin expansion of 320 basis points. Additionally, both the triple-net and outpatient medical results came in steady, with outpatient NOI inching 2%, while the triple-net portfolio's coverage jumped above pre-pandemic levels.

On top of that, Welltower continues to invest in new properties, expanding its market share further. Gross activity numbers during the year came in at a whopping $7 billion, with $6 billion going into acquisitions, while it added 100 new properties. Moreover, it has already secured $2 billion in transactions within the first few months of the year. Also, given its pristine balance sheet, I won't be surprised if it maintains that same momentum this year. With a net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA of 3.5 times, along with $9 billion of liquidity, Welltower has enough firepower for future dealmaking.

Furthermore, Gurufocus' financial strength rating for the stock adds even more credibility to the argument. With a Debt‐ to‐ Equity of 0.52 and an Equity‐ to‐ Asset ratio of 0.63, we can see that the stock relies moderately on leverage along with a healthy proportion of equity in its capital stack. Similarly, its interest coverage ratio of two points to a robust ability to service debt. Also, with an Altman Z-score above three, Welltower is moving toward the "Safe" zone, which shows a low risk of bankruptcy.

Price vs. Value: Breaking Down Welltower's Valuation

Welltower stock has staged a tremendous rally in recent quarters, though its longer-term performance (going back to 2020), trails the S&P 500 (67.9% total return vs. 80.9%). In the past year though, the stock has leapfrogged over 60%, vastly ahead of the sector median's high-single-digit gains. In the past six months alone, the stock has jumped almost 28%, while the healthcare REIT's average posted a relatively modest decline.

However, the real story is the stock's recent price surge, leaving its valuation metrics considerably higher compared to sector averages. It's currently trading hands for a trailing PE ratio above 100, against a sector median that's closer to 31. On a forward basis, the stock trades close to 87 times earnings, which is more than double the broader market's benchmark of 34 times. Also, its price-to-cash flow ratio of 42.6 times comes in firmly ahead of the sector median of 13.3 times.

Moreover, in assessing the stock's value, I ran a DVM model, which signals a deep disconnect between WELL stock's intrinsic value and its current price near $150. We can see that with a fairly gracious assumption of an 8% dividend growth over the next three years, and a long-term expansion of 4%, we arrive at a fair value of close to $112. Judging from its current price of close to $150, that implies a downside of almost 30%.

I call it a gracious assumption because Welltower's dividend profile is mostly underwhelming. Its forward dividend yield of around 1.8% comfortably lags the healthcare REIT average which is often above 4%. Also, its historical 5-year dividend growth rate is at a negative 6%, which highlights the decline from its pre-cut dividend level. On the positive side, Welltower's forward payout ratio is at 60%, which is relatively conservative compared to REIT standards. Also, despite the sluggishness in payout growth, it has however paid a dividend for almost 35 years.

Risks To The Thesis

Though Welltower stock appears to have little margin of safety at current prices, compelling counterarguments suggest it could climb even higher. I guess the primary element that underpins its bull case centers around its demographic inevitability, with a wave of retirees that've reached their 80s. At the current pace, it seems plausible that senior housing demand will outstrip supply resulting in multiple years of above-average rent expansion, occupancy gains, and NOI growth.

Also, the company's management has proven adept at capital allocation, seizing organic expansion opportunities at the right time. Its leadership has shown a knack for efficiently recycling capital into higher-returning assets while offloading or transitioning underperforming ones to more capable partners, unlocking hidden NOI potential. At the same time, it continues investing in technology and platform upgrades to continue bolstering efficiency and resident satisfaction, driving better margins and longer length-of-stay. Moreover, given its size and reputation, Welltower has the edge in deal flow while making it a partner of choice for property developers.

Takeaway On Welltower Stock

Welltower has been on a killer run for all the right reasons, but it seems it has run a tad too far. Its operational performance remains strong, supported by long-term growth tailwinds. However, it is important to note that all those positives have apparently been priced in, leaving little to no margin of safety. Hence, it's better to wait for a more attractive entry point, before adding more of the stock.

data.title: Oddity Tech: A Phenomenal Small-Cap Play
data.description:
Business Overview

Oddity Tech (NASDAQ:ODD) is an Israeli-based beauty tech business. The business currently has two brands, IL MAKIAGE and Spoiled Child, which they sell to consumers completely online. Oddity leverages innovative technology within their websites to streamline the process of buying beauty products online and give consumers more confidence.

Investment Thesis

My investment thesis in Oddity Tech can be summarized into 4 key points.

Role As a Disruptor

The role they serve is as a disruptor within the beauty business. Currently, only 25% of the beauty business is online, with the other 75% occurring in person.

The problem that Oddity solves is easing the transition from in-person to online shopping for beauty through their brands IL MAKIAGE and Spoiled Child. For example, when you go in person to buy beauty products, you have the ability to talk to the employees at the store, speak with your friends and peers, and ultimately feel very confident about the decision you make to buy the product. However, when shopping online, consumers are typically thrown into the same large catalog of products with no help. Oddity, realizing this problem, solves it by implementing various AI technologies and a unique UI for their website, which gives consumers confidence in the purchases they make online. Although other beauty companies have similar tech to Oddity on their websites, they don’t emphasize their technology focus enough, which leaves them at a major disadvantage.

For instance, 40% of Oddity’s headcount consists of tech employees, and the company also places a large emphasis on gathering and utilizing data. On the other hand, most beauty companies place little emphasis on technology, which leads them to develop tech slowly and fail to realize the full benefits of the data they have at their disposal.

Loved by Consumers

IL MAKIAGE and Spoiled Child are loved by consumers. When buying a company at the intersection of tech and consumer, the #1 concern is the degree to which consumers like their product. When discussing this business with others, the primary concerns that arise are related to people's anecdotal experiences with the company or personal concerns about the makeup products. However, numbers do not lie. In the past year, approximately 50% of Oddity’s revenues came from repeat customers. Furthermore, they have a 120% repeat rate in their latest quarter, meaning that if a customer buys $100 worth of products when they first visit the store, they purchase an additional $120 worth of products over the next 12 months—an almost unheard-of repeat rate, which has increased 6x since the company started. Oddity has leveraged the flywheel effect of data to better curate their products for consumers and bring them back to the business.

DTC advantage

Oddity is completely DTC. Through their brands, they acquire and sell to customers 100% online, something completely unseen in the beauty industry. Through Oddity’s strategy of avoiding wholesalers, they benefit in several ways: 1)They are less prone to boom-and-bust cycles since wholesalers don’t account for a large portion of inventory purchases. 2)They can better control the reputation and image of their product. Building on the fact that Oddity is completely DTC, they have also fully in-housed their marketing.

Honest and Able Management

An acute ability to control their growth and honest management. The reason Oddity is the most unique company I have ever seen is that it is the first business I’ve found that purposely didn’t grow as much as it could in a given year. Let me explain. Oddity, which is founder-led (the CEO owns 30% of common stock), believes in a long-term growth algorithm of 20% revenue growth and EBITDA margins. The company laid out this plan because they want to grow sustainably for a long time, building the foundation to become a true beauty giant. Interestingly, management is extremely committed to this vision.

In Q1 2024, they said some remarkable things in their earnings call: "In Q1 2023, I made a mistake and grew too much. We grew 83%, which led to an insane growth of 57% for the full year, and we decided not to do it again, although I strongly believe we could. The goal for the long term is, again, as we mentioned before, 20% plus and 20% EBITDA margin, and by pacing the growth, I'm ensuring it will happen and will continue to be in the way. For Q1, we built the model and managed the growth to support 22% to 24% year-over-year, and I will say it again, pacing growth is my decision. But even when we are pacing the growth to 20% plus, we are growing way more than my legacy competitors, which are in single digits, and we are still securing the business early in the year. Age one, it's still the most important period for us." (Q1 2024 Earnings Call)

This excerpt from the earnings call is remarkable for a few reasons. First, management has a tremendous interest in the company’s success, given the large amount of stock they own, yet they still choose to commit to their long-term growth algorithm, which almost certainly limits short-term gains in the stock. Evidently, the management team, particularly the CEO, possesses tremendous integrity and honesty—key traits to consider when looking to take a sizable position in a business. Then, elaborating on what they mean by “limiting” their growth, Oddity’s in-house marketing allows them to track and adjust their growth pace. When they begin tracking above their 20% top-line growth target, they can easily tone down their ad spend, which often happens due to the business's phenomenal ROAS.

Overall, this is a phenomenal business, trading at just 16x TEV/EBITDA, with no debt and excess cash, repurchasing shares, and with long-term potential for continued revenue growth and margin expansion. Overall, this business appears poised to turn in a beauty giant.

Competitive Edge

The only beauty business with a focus on tech. Although other companies have developed similar technologies, the pace at which Oddity innovates and leverages data is unmatched. Most beauty companies are formulated with a beauty-first vision, making it hard to properly implement tech and take advantage of data. Furthermore, although beauty products tend to be non-discretionary and “sticky” in nature, Oddity’s repeat rates demonstrate the high degree to which they can keep users coming back. The fact that Oddity has some of the best technology for consumers to make purchases online makes it extremely difficult for those consumers to confidently purchase other beauty products completely DTC. Lastly, Oddity operates solely through its DTC segment, which ensures they can better maintain their image relative to other beauty brands that rely on wholesale and allows them to have greater control over inventory dynamics.

Growth Opportunities

New Brand Launches: Oddity is launching two new brands in the second half of 2025, covering the telehealth space and another unknown category. These brands will be integral in laying the foundation for their business far into the future as they expand into major beauty categories.

International Expansion: About 19% of Oddity’s revenues come from outside the U.S., yet this number has fallen over the past few years. Management has realized that there is still tremendous growth potential within the U.S., so they have de-emphasized international expansion (which has already seen tremendous success) to fully capitalize on the U.S. market before leveraging international growth.

Scaling Existing Brands: Oddity has seen phenomenal ROAS when advertising for IL MAKIAGE and Spoiled Child, leading to their explosive growth. However, these brands are still relatively small and have an extremely long runway for future growth.

Model

When modelling Oddity, I leveraged a basic DCF where I inputted their TTM FCF, approximate growth rates for next 10 years as outlaid by management, a generous 20 exit multiple to reflect the growth and moat of the business, as well as a discount rate of 15% which is close to their WACC when using an equity risk premium of 7.5%.

Overall, even with very conservative assumptions, Oddity has upside of around 40% at today's price to generate a strong 15% compounded annualized return for the next 10 years.

Potential Risks

A large portion of Oddity’s marketing is likely done on TikTok. A permanent TikTok ban could harm the high returns on ad spend the business currently enjoys. In addition, A major impairment to the reputation of their products could skew consumer preferences. Overall, I view the potential risks for this business to be very limited to the relatively non-discretionary nature of beauty products as well as the businesses position as a disruptor within the space.

Final Thoughts

Overall, I believe Oddity is a rare and phenomenal opportunity within the small-cap realm. The market is overly pessimistic due to a slowdown in bottom line growth in 2025 as a result of inflated expenses from the launch of new brands and the company itself being a relatively recent IPO. However, I believe Oddity is the perfect representation of a wonderful business at a fair price which makes a phenomenal strong buying opportunity to hold for a very long period of time.

data.title: U.K. Services Inflation Lower Than Expected In Boost For Bank of England
data.description:
By James Smith

Let’s start with the bad news: UK headline inflation is back at 3%, up from 2.5%, after briefly dipping below the Bank of England’s 2% target last Autumn. That’s a tad higher than expected, though almost entirely because of a near-1% month-on-month increase in food prices, which is hard to explain. Even so, headline CPI is poised to remain in the 3% region for much of this year, and we expect a peak of 3.5% later this year. Much of this can be traced back to household energy bills, which are set to increase again in April due to rising wholesale natural gas prices.

But energy and food are of little relevance to the BoE’s decision-making. What really matters is service sector inflation, and here the news is getting better. Admittedly, services CPI did rebound up to 5%, though that was lower than expected and followed an artificially low reading in December. Airfares didn’t properly account for the usual surge in prices around Christmas.

Again, though, airfares don’t matter for monetary policy. And once you strip out the volatile items as well as rents, ‘core services’ inflation is falling. There isn't a single official measure of this, but when we calculate it, we tend to strip out things like airfares, package holidays, and also rents. Rental growth has been relentless, but the Bank of England doesn't seem to put much weight on it.

By our calculations, that measure of ‘core services’ inflation now sits at 4.2%, down from 4.7% two months ago.

UK services inflation measures

We expect this trend to continue. We think the measure of ‘core services’ can dip below 4% within the next couple of months, while overall services inflation could be there by the summer. Crucially, that’s a faster fall the Bank of England is currently forecasting. Huge chunks of the services basket are subject to annual price hikes in April, which owing to lower headline inflation, should be less aggressive than they were in April 2024.

If we’re right, that wouldn’t necessarily speed up the pace of rate cuts, but it would help cement a total of four cuts this year. We also expect rates to fall to 3.25% in 2026, which is a fair bit lower than markets are currently pricing.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Arista Networks Earnings: Not Enough Upside For This Inflection Investor
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Arista Networks (NYSE:ANET) (NEOE:ANET:CA) delivered an impressive end to an impressive year, with Q4 2024 results that were astonishing. And yet, the stock is down slightly after hours.

And the reason is that investors' expectations got ahead of themselves. More specifically, I make the case that the pace of improvement in profitability already took place in ANET's 2024 banner year.

And that now, the stock is more fairly priced and not as enticing as it was.

More specifically, I find that paying 34x forward non-GAAP operating profits leaves ANET fairly priced.

In other words, there is a lot to like about ANET's story, but is there enough new news to inflect this stock higher? I'm not entirely convinced.

Rapid Recap

Recall, ANET is a stock that DVR used to own and recommend to its paying subscribers, and we walked away with a 50%+ return, see below.

Then, last month, I downgraded ANET to neutral and said,

On the one hand, the market sell-off has already significantly built in a discount into Arista Networks' prospects, so selling now, defeats the now built-in margin of safety.

On the other hand, the stock isn't so cheap that it makes this opportunity a no-brainer.

On balance, I argue that the stock is fairly valued, with notable risks to think about.

The graphic that follows is a recap of my journey with ANET.

With that in mind, here's why I'm now neutral on ANET.

Arista Networks' Near-Term Prospects

Arista Networks makes high-performance networking products that power data centers and AI infrastructure.

Their main hardware products are switches and routers that help hyperscaleres like Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), and Oracle (ORCL) move massive amounts of data rapidly and efficiently.

Simply put, ANET builds the highways for the internet's biggest tech-titan workloads, making sure everything runs smoothly and at top speed.

During the earnings call, as you'd expect, we heard a lot about the demand for AI computing.

The demand for faster, more reliable AI workloads, is growing like crazy, and Arista is right in the middle of it. Of course, much of this was known and mostly already priced.

What was interesting to hear was management's confidence around DeepSeek and how this is driving a need for more powerful networking solutions.

As you know, AI models require huge amounts of data to be processed, and any slowdowns in networking can bottleneck performance.

Moreover, given that ANET's strength is in AI networking, this makes ANET nicely positioned to handle the massive increase in AI demand.

As AI workloads get even more complex, Arista's technology will be critical in keeping things running efficiently. The rise of AI also means more companies will prioritize high-performance networking infrastructure, which plays right into Arista's strengths.

Given this background, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates Could Reach 20%

In my previous analysis, I said,

I suspect that when 2025 is done and dusted, [ANET's] revenue growth rates will probably be similar to 2024, particularly given that the comparables with 2024 are so easy.

ANET is guiding for about 17%, which means that it should at least deliver 20% topline growth. This means that my instincts for ANET's prospects were on the right track.

Obviously, I can't predict exactly how its growth rates will unfold, but the fact of the matter remains that being vaguely right is a lot better than being precise and wrong.

Given this context, let's now delve into its valuation.

ANET Stock Valuation -- 32x Forward Non-GAAP Operating Profits

As an Inflection investor, the crown jewel of my bull case for ANET had centered and continues to center around the fact that ANET operates with no debt. This is just remarkable and provides management massive flexibility.

On top of that, consider that ANET ended Q4 2024 with approximately $8.2 billion of cash and marketable securities. This means that 6% of its market cap is made up of net cash, and thereby provides this investment thesis with a wide margin of safety, and something that makes me potentially reconsider ANET at the right level of investors' expectations.

Now, one key aspect that does make me somewhat wary of ANET is the fact that its non-GAAP operating margins appear to be somewhat compressing. Having ended the first 9 months of 2024 with non-GAAP operating margins of 47.7%, its Q4 2024 non-GAAP operating margins stood at 47.0%.

And then, its guidance for Q4 points to at least 44%. Even if ANET's non-GAAP operating margins end Q1 2025 at 46%, it clearly plays into the idea that the best of its near-term improvement in profitability may have already taken place in 2024.

Along these lines, let's make the case that for 2025, ANET's non-GAAP operating margins reach around 46%. And then, let's also assume that ANET's revenues reach $8.8 billion, $600 million higher than the high end of management's guidance for $8.2 billion.

This means that if ANET flawlessly executes, it will deliver $4.1 billion of non-GAAP operating profits. And while that's an impressive improvement of 40% over the $2.9 billion delivered in 2024, we have to think about how much of this underlying profitability isn't already priced in?

Basically, having to pay 32x forward non-GAAP operating profits doesn't entice me to get involved with ANET.

Risk Factors to the Upside

The right stock in the right sector with the right story and no valuation is too high. We've seen this time and time and time again over the years, from Tesla (TSLA) to Netflix (NFLX) and now recently with Palantir (PLTR). These names were always viewed as hyper-expensive only to continue to move towards even more expensive, and the market was completely right to have been bullish on these stocks.

And there's no doubt that ANET is incredibly well positioned, so even though paying 32x forward non-GAAP operating profits prices me out of this stock, I fully recognize that this is probably to do with the fact that I know I can find a better risk-reward elsewhere.

The other aspect to think about here is that it would not be at all problematic for ANET to leverage its balance sheet with some debt and make a bold needle-moving acquisition that could easily further ignite its growth opportunity.

After all, that was exactly Deep Value Returns' playbook with Broadcom (AVGO) in 2023, before they acquired VMware, and that turned out to be a very strong setup for the bulls.

The Bottom Line

While Arista Networks remains a strong player in AI networking, much of its near-term growth and profitability improvements are already reflected in the stock price.

The company's 2024 was a banner year, but with operating margins showing signs of compression and forward earnings priced at 32x non-GAAP operating profits, the setup lacks the asymmetric upside that an Inflection investor would take.

While ANET may continue performing well, the risk-reward balance appears too even to justify me getting bullish right now. I could be wrong, but I'll just cheer the bulls from the sidelines.

data.title: Neste's Market Free-Fall May Continue
data.description:
On the face of it, Neste Oyj (OTCPK:NTOIY) (OTCPK:NTOIF) offers an attractive investment case: it is a first mover in the provision of renewable fuels and circular solutions, it enjoys scale advantages, and it is clearly growing. Yet, profitability is challenged, and the firm faces significant headwinds. As a result, the firm has a negative intrinsic value and the market has punished it, with the stock price declining by nearly 64% in the last year. Investors should avoid this dangerous stock.

The Business Model

Founded in Finland, Neste’s roots are in traditional oil refining, but the company has turned itself into a global leader in the transition from fossil fuels to renewables and circular solutions. The company’s status as one of the world's leading producers of renewable diesel and sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), highlights the success of its evolution. The company specializes in refining waste, residues, and innovative raw materials into high-quality renewable fuels. Neste operates in three primary business segments: Renewable Products, Oil Products, and Marketing & Services, and Others, with the Renewable Products segment being the company’s core growth engine. The Renewable Products segment benefits from high global demand for renewable diesel and SAF.

The company’s ability to develop and scale its renewable solutions is critical to its ability to grow and earn a profit. Its proprietary NEXBTL technology enables the conversion of waste, residues, and novel raw materials into high-quality renewable diesel and other low-emission fuels. This is the company’s key differentiator as it competes with traditional oil refiners, and it also allows it to compete for an emerging customer base that seeks cleaner energy solutions. In addition, the firm has built a robust, global supply chain for renewable raw materials, which allows it to scale production without too much reliance on conventional biofuel feedstocks such as palm oil. In fact, by 2023, waste and residues were responsible for 92% of Neste’s renewable raw material inputs, demonstrating the company’s commitment to sustainability and resource efficiency.

Neste enjoys economies of scale as the largest global producer of SAF and renewable diesel. As you will see later, this has allowed the company to maintain a premium on its margins. Neste’s combination of an extensive refining network, spanning Finland, the Netherlands, and Singapore, enables it to serve a geographically diverse market that encompasses North America, Europe, and the Asia-Pacific, de-risking the business model from region-specific economic downturns. The company’s solutions are well in line with regulatory and market trends toward low-carbon energy solutions. This has created a healthy tailwind that has driven growth in Neste’s business.

Given these trends, Neste is likely to continue growing in the short to medium term, especially as the aviation, road transport, and industrial sectors are particularly affected by regulatory efforts toward decarbonization. Furthermore, many corporations have maintained aggressive net-zero targets, further adding to the attractiveness of Neste’s market. Neste’s investments into expanding its refining capacity - such as its recent Singapore refinery expansion and ongoing Rotterdam refinery upgrades-, will increase its competitive positioning for the years ahead. Management believes that by 2026, Neste will have a nameplate capacity of 6.8 million tons of renewable products per year.

Core Profitability is Declining

Neste is growing strongly, with revenue nearly doubling between 2020 and 2024, from €10.4 billion to €€20.7 billion. However, revenue in the trailing twelve months (TTM) declined by 9.8% from 2023 levels, due to challenging market conditions and lower refining margins. Furthermore, this is a business that demands very large outlays for materials and supplies, so that gross profits are fairly thin, with gross margins averaging 17.4% in the 2020-2024 period. The Renewable Products segment is the company’s largest and most profitable division, generating comparable EBITDA of €514 million in 2024, although this is materially down from €1.9 billion in 2023. Margins are at their highest in the Renewable Products segment, with a comparable EBITDA margin of around 25%, much higher than the Oil Products segment, which has lower margins thanks to the more volatile nature of fossil fuel markets and the comparable lack of pricing power. The Marketing & Services segment, the smallest of the segments, is a source of stable cash flows and supports the company’s distribution network in Finland and the Baltic region.

In 2024, the Renewable Products division experienced a decline in its comparable sales margin to 377 USD/ton in 2024, compared to 863 USD/ton in the previous year, largely because of market overcapacity and weaker pricing power. Nevertheless, total renewable sales volumes grew to 3,729 kilotons, up from 3,382 kilotons in 2023, driven by growing SAF sales, which reached 412 kilotons compared to 139 kilotons in 2023. Although 2024 was a challenging period, the company was still able to maintain a margin premium over reference markets through strong customer relationships and successful term deals.

The Oil Products segment has been challenged as well, with total refining margin falling to 14.1 USD/bbl from 21.1 USD/bbl in 2023. Although operational performance was strong, the fall in product cracks reduced profitability, with comparable EBITDA for the segment falling to €633 million, from €1.4 billion in 2023. The Marketing & Services segment was typically stable, generating a comparable EBITDA of €101 million compared to €118 million in 2023.

Crucially, the company’s core profitability declined, with net operating profit after tax (NOPAT) falling from €1.4 billion in 2023 to just €113 million in 2024,although this is higher than the company’s reported net income of -€95 million, as the table below shows.

Source: Author calculations and company filings

As a result, Neste’s return on capital employed (ROACE) dropped below its target level. The company was able to maintain financial flexibility thanks to its leverage ratio remaining below the 40% threshold. , providing financial flexibility. Neste implemented a €350 million performance improvement program to improve cost efficiency by 2026. As the company continues to grow, management signalled that capex would be somewhere in the region of €1.1 billion and €1.3 billion in 2025, as the company attempts to increase renewable production capacity and maintain its operational reliability.

Headwinds Are Forming

Regulatory uncertainty has increased in recent years, first with pressure in Europe for continued growth and second, with the Trump Administration’s shift away from a federal level push toward decarbonization. With the global economy likely to contract in the event of tariff wars, pressure to return to fossil fuels will be great. This is significant because, for example, in the European Union, SAF mandates are a source of growth, so that, if they were removed, Neste’s business would be severely impacted.

Secondly, the company faces fierce competition for sustainable feedstocks, which could put its margins under pressure if they cannot secure large amounts of cost-effective feedstocks.

Furthermore, traditional oil majors such as Chevron (CVX), Exxon Mobil (XOM), and BP (BP) have begun to see the opportunity in becoming renewable fuels and specialized biofuel producers. This is likely to impact margins in biofuels and synthetic fuels. These companies have already established refining infrastructure and distribution networks that they can and already used to compete with Neste. Although Neste has a first-mover advantage, it will be hard to compete with these more resourced firms.

Valuation

To calculate Neste’s intrinsic value, we capitalise its TTM NOPAT by its weighted average cost of capital (WACC) of 5.31%, and then add its excess capital of €79 million, add its net deferred tax liability of €113 million, and deduct the fair value of its total debt of €€4.4 billion, we get an intrinsic value of €2.4 billion, far below the company’s market cap of $8.2 billion. This reflects the decline in NOPAT, and it is not surprising that the market has hammered the stock price, with the share price falling some 64% in the last year. Until the company can get back to its NOPAT levels, the market will continue to mark down the value of the company. Investors in Neste will be punished.

Conclusion

On paper, Neste presents a strong investment case, but in practice, and as the company’s financial statements show, it has been hard to translate the promise of the business into an investment case. Although the company enjoys scale economies, has valuable proprietary technology, and is investing wisely in expanding its lead in the industry, profitability has been challenged. At present, the company has a negative intrinsic value and the market has recognised this. Investors should stay away.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
